In Exodus 10, we encounter the relentless cycle of judgment and resistance that defines Pharaoh's conflict with God. The chapter opens with an unsettling statement: the LORD has hardened Pharaoh's heart so that He might display His miraculous power and ensure that future generations would know His name and authority. This is not mere narrative—it is a declaration of God's purpose woven through calamity. Moses stands before Pharaoh again with increasing urgency, not pleading but warning of the eighth plague: locusts that will consume everything the hail left behind. Yet what strikes us is Pharaoh's fractured stubbornness. His own officials plead with him to relent, acknowledging that "Egypt lies in ruins," but he refuses. When he finally seems to yield—"Go, worship the LORD your God"—he immediately qualifies it, granting permission only to the men while holding the women, children, and livestock hostage. He is negotiating with God as though he retains leverage.

When the locusts descend, the devastation is absolute and unprecedented. Moses stretches out his staff, an east wind carries them across Egypt, and by morning the sky is black with them. They consume every green thing, completing the destruction that the hail began. For the first time, Pharaoh confesses genuine sin: "I have sinned against the LORD your God and against you." Yet his words ring hollow. He asks for forgiveness and appeals to Moses to remove the plague, and Moses intercedes—the LORD sends a powerful west wind that sweeps the locusts into the Red Sea. Not a single one remains. But the text then delivers a blow: "The LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he would not let the Israelites go." Forgiveness was granted, relief was delivered, and Pharaoh's heart turned to stone anyway.

The ninth plague—three days of absolute darkness—follows immediately. For three days, Egyptians cannot see their own hands before their faces, cannot move from their places. Yet all the Israelites have light where they dwell. This is no accident of nature; it is a demonstration that God's judgment is precise, His protection particular. Pharaoh summons Moses one more time and concedes further ground: the women and children may go too. But Moses, speaking with prophetic clarity, demands that the livestock go as well—they will need them for worship, and they will not know what form that worship will take until they arrive at the place God shows them. Pharaoh refuses. He has reached the limit of his concession, the boundary of his pride.

What we witness in this chapter is not negotiation but revelation. God is displaying His absolute sovereignty over creation, over nations, over human hearts. The hardening of Pharaoh's heart is not arbitrary cruelty; it is the exposure of what was already there—a will set against God's word, a pride that counts the ruin of a nation as preferable to obedience. Pharaoh's confessions prove hollow because confession without surrender is mere performance. He will repent when the pain comes, but he will not obey when the judgment lifts. And God, in His wisdom, allows this pattern to continue so that His name might be known throughout all generations—not as a tyrant, but as the God who will ultimately free His people and vindicate His word. The chapter ends with Pharaoh's final ultimatum: never appear before him again. What he means as rejection, God accepts as prelude.
